one_line_takeaway: ASTRA turns black-box jailbreaking into a continuously-learning search: by scoring every attack on a 1-10 scale and distilling each interaction into a retrievable Effective/Promising/Ineffective strategy, it hits 80.6% average ASR on HarmBench at 2.4 queries/success, outperforming prior automated attacks by ~20 points while remaining robust to standard defenses.
key_finding: On HarmBench (400 textual-behavior queries) ASTRA reaches an average ASR of 80.6% across 8 target models, beating the second-best baseline (CodeAttack, 60.0%) by 20.6 percentage points, while being the most query-efficient (average 2.4 queries per successful jailbreak vs 2.7 for ReNeLLM/CipherChat and 5.5 for PAIR). It is near-total against several models (Gemini-2.0-Flash 98.5%, DeepSeek-R1 95.5%, GPT-4o 93.8%, GPT-4.1 91.0%) but far weaker against Claude-3.7-Sonnet (36.0%). Ablations show retrieval is the single most important component (removing it drops the 5-model-subset average from 71.9% to 48.5%), followed by the Effective library (58.6%) and the fine-grained scoring vs binary (62.1%). Distilled strategies transfer: frozen HarmBench libraries retain high ASR on AdvBench-50 (e.g., 94.0% Gemini-2.0-Flash, 42.0% Claude-3.7-Sonnet) and across different attacker models (Qwen3-32B, GPT-4o, Gemini-2-Flash). ASTRA stays robust under defenses, only modestly degrading against Llama Guard 3 (GPT-4o 93.8% -> 86.3%; Gemini-2.5-Flash 86.0% -> 78.0%).
core_contribution: ASTRA is a black-box automated LLM jailbreak framework whose novelty is a closed-loop 'attack-evaluate-distill-reuse' mechanism that gives the attacker continuous-learning / self-evolution capability. Its distinguishing contribution over prior automated attacks (PAIR, TAP, fuzzers) is that instead of treating each attack outcome as binary success/failure and discarding it, ASTRA distills every interaction into a reusable, structured strategy and accumulates knowledge across attacks. Concretely it introduces (1) a fine-grained scalar reward (a Judge score s in [1,10]) used as a 'reward-shaping' signal so the search gets dense feedback instead of a sparse binary reward; (2) a Strategy Extractor that turns each interaction into a JSON strategy object with description/guide/examples, conditioned on the score; and (3) a dynamic three-tier strategy library -- Effective (s=10), Promising (5<s<10), Ineffective (s<=5) -- indexed by an embedding of the harmful question and retrieved via cosine similarity to guide future attacks. The three cooperating modules are the Attack Designer, the Strategy Extractor, and the Strategy Storage & Retrieval module.
threat_model: Black-box attacker with only input-output (API/web) access to a target LLM; no access to weights, gradients, or internal activations (explicitly contrasted with white-box/optimization attacks like GCG that need internal states). The attacker's goal is to craft prompts that elicit content violating the target's safety policy. The attacker additionally controls two auxiliary LLMs it queries freely: an Attacker/Extractor model (GLM-4.5) that generates prompts and distills strategies, and a Judge model (GPT-4o) that scores harmfulness. Attack budget is capped at N=10 queries per harmful question. The threat model also covers deployed defenses: the paper evaluates against Paraphrase, Perplexity Filter, Llama Guard 3, and the OpenAI Moderation endpoint.
limitations: Author-stated: high internal computational overhead -- coordinating Attack Designer, Extractor, and Judge requires continuous multi-turn inference, raising latency and token cost vs static template attacks, and the initial strategy-library build-up is resource-intensive (they suggest distilled/lightweight models for intermediate steps). Reviewer-noted: results are entirely dependent on GPT-4o-as-judge with success defined as a single score of 10, so ASR reflects that judge's harmfulness rubric rather than verified real-world harm; the strongest target (Claude-3.7-Sonnet, 36%) shows the method is far from universal; efficiency (AQ) is computed only over successful queries, so it understates true cost on hard targets; no human validation of the '400 harmful' successes is reported; and the paper does not test adaptive defenses that specifically target the accumulated-strategy mechanism.

datasets_used: HarmBench textual-behaviors subset (400 harmful queries) -- primary evaluation
AdvBench-50 (50 harmful queries) -- cross-dataset transferability test
quantitative_results: ASTRA average ASR on HarmBench (8 models): 80.6%, +20.6 pp over second-best baseline
Per-target ASR: Llama-3-8B 54.5%, Llama-3-70B 89.3%, DeepSeek-R1 95.5%, GPT-4o 93.8%, GPT-4.1 91.0%, Gemini-2.0-Flash 98.5%, Gemini-2.5-Flash 86.0%, Claude-3.7-Sonnet 36.0%
ASTRA average AQ (queries per successful attack): 2.4 -- lowest of all methods (PAIR 5.5, TAP 6.3, GPTFuzzer 4.9, ReNeLLM 2.7, CipherChat 2.7, CodeAttack 3.1)

Ablation avg ASR over 5-model subset: full ASTRA 71.9%; w/o Effective library 58.6%; w/o Promising 65.3%; w/o Ineffective 65.4%; w/o Retrieval 48.5% (largest drop); w/o fine-grained Score (binary) 62.1%; w/o Extractor (raw logs) 61.7%
Cross-dataset transfer to AdvBench-50 (frozen HarmBench library): Gemini-2.0-Flash 98.5%->94.0%, GPT-4o 93.8%->88.0%, Claude-3.7-Sonnet 36.0%->42.0%, DeepSeek-R1 95.5%->93.3%
Defense robustness (ASR, GPT-4o / Gemini-2.5-Flash): No Defense 93.8/86.0, Paraphrase 92.5/85.0, Perplexity Filter 93.3/83.8, Llama Guard 3 86.3/78.0, OpenAI Moderation 88.0/81.3
Retrieval count k sweep: ASR rises with k then plateaus, e.g. GPT-4o 60.8% (k small) up to ~93.8% at k=9; default k=9 (top-3 per library tier)
Success threshold: Judge score s=10 required to count as a jailbreak; attack budget N=10 per query
